summary: McKenzie outlines his conversion funnel strategy for Bingo Card Creator, moving visitors from free PDF downloads to trial software to paid purchases. He details five specific trial limitations designed to let users verify the product works while making it insufficient for actual classroom use. He explains why a usage-based limit (number of cards) works better than a time-based trial for his product, since the value proposition is saving time during recurring classroom needs. He also makes the case for offering an unconditional money-back guarantee, arguing it costs almost nothing while reducing purchase anxiety for buyers dealing with an unknown internet vendor.
key_points: Free resources serve as top-of-funnel content that drives organic search traffic into a natural progression toward the paid product

Trial limitations should be designed around the product's core value proposition — for a classroom tool, limiting card count below class size makes the trial verifiably functional but practically useless
A money-back guarantee costs nothing when you're not making sales, and roughly 10% of one sale's profit when you are — a trivial cost that reduces purchase friction
Most dissatisfied customers self-select during the free trial, so refund requests remain rare by design
Social psychology works in the seller's favor: most people feel deeply uncomfortable asking for refunds, making guarantee abuse a non-issue
money_quotes: You can't go to your room of 25 3rd graders and say 'Sorry kids, I know we were supposed to play bingo today but you're going to have to share 15 cards'.
A guarantee costs me absolutely nothing if I'm not already making money.
Every time they are in a crisis and turn to my program for a fun, rewarding lesson whipped up in a jiffy, thats another opportunity for me to make a sale.
The maximally finicky ones will self-select away from sending me money.
Handing over your money to a no-name stranger on the Internet can be a scary experience. The guarantee reduces the perceived risk of that action to very close to zero.
If it motivates more than 1 marginal person to convert for every 10 people who excercize their right to return the software, the guarantee has made me money.
key_insight: Trial limitations should be calibrated so the product demonstrably works but remains practically useless for the customer's real need, creating natural purchase pressure without antagonizing users.
